title: IAG report reveals generational economic strain
full_text:
IAG report reveals resilient nation overall — but generational divides
IAG’s new report reveals Australians are cautiously optimistic despite mounting pressures, but a widening generational divide in resilience is testing the nation’s capacity to adapt to increasing disruption.
Advertiser content for IAG
Rain clears and recovery begins for Western Sydney floods following the 1-in-50 year flood event in March.
New research from IAG, Australia and New Zealand’s largest general insurer, reveals Australians remain cautiously optimistic about the nation’s future. Despite nearly two-thirds experiencing significant disruption in the past year from intensifying cost-of-living pressures, climate volatility and economic uncertainty, 58 per cent of consumers and 72 per cent of SMEs express confidence.
These findings are part of the inaugural IAG Resilient Futures Report, which introduces the IAG Risk Index – a new national benchmark tracking Australians’ confidence, outlook and exposure to disruption.
Insights from over 2,400 consumers and small businesses gathered by IAG (whose brands include NRMA Insurance, WFI, RACQ Insurance and ROLLiN), highlight cost-of-living pressure as the single biggest concern. It is influencing daily spending, financial security and preparedness, and revealing generational differences in risk perception.
Climate-related disruption is also a prominent business risk, with nearly half of small businesses (48 per cent) impacted by extreme weather in the past year. Gaps persist in business continuity planning, backup suppliers and insurance review.
Younger Australians, particularly Gen Z and Millennials, show more hope but greater financial exposure. To manage rising costs, many rely on debt (45.5 per cent of Millennials), delay healthcare (47.7 per cent) or take on extra work (60.5 per cent of Gen Z).
Older Australians, especially Baby Boomers, show lower optimism (46.1 per cent versus 56.7 per cent nationally) but possess more financial buffers and take protective steps, highlighting a growing resilience gap.
IAG CEO Nick Hawkins observed that despite Australian optimism, broader macroeconomic headwinds, particularly from the Middle East, are further straining household budgets.
IAG CEO Nick Hawkins: “Strengthening Australia’s resilience means helping people better anticipate disruption and recover more quickly.”
“These global dynamics are now shaping Australians’ behaviour – constraining spending, delaying investment and eroding savings. This is particularly acute among younger people and smaller businesses with less capacity to absorb sustained pressure,” he noted.
Mr Hawkins added the Report and IAG Risk Index aim to inform organisations responding to economic pressure.
“Australia’s future prosperity depends on how well we anticipate and recover from disruption,” he said. “The Resilient Futures Report and IAG Risk Index provide a clear picture of how Australians are feeling about risk and where protection gaps remain.”
Nationwide resilience varies significantly
Consumer resilience shows clear regional divides. New South Wales (61.6 per cent) and Western Australia (60.3 per cent) consumers are most positive nationally. Victoria lags at 54 per cent, Queensland aligns with the national average (58.5 per cent), and South Australia, while near average sentiment (57.7 per cent), has the weakest outlook (53.1 per cent).
Risk is becoming harder to manage
Risk is no longer a distant concern, with 67 per cent of consumers and 64 per cent of SMEs experiencing significant disruption in the past year. Beyond dominant cost-of-living pressures, Australians face a broader mix of challenges – from cyber threats and mental wellbeing to personal safety and extreme weather – making disruption more frequent and harder to manage. Despite heightened awareness, many struggle to strengthen resilience due to financial pressure, competing priorities and uncertainty about effective actions.
Mr Hawkins stated the findings reveal a growing gap between Australians’ understanding of risk and their capacity to respond.
“Risk is no longer hypothetical; it is something many are experiencing in real time,” he said. “The IAG Risk Index shows that while risk awareness is high, preparedness is not always keeping pace. Strengthening Australia’s resilience means helping people better anticipate disruption, prepare and recover more quickly.”
The IAG Resilient Futures Report draws on insights from a nationally representative survey of more than 2,400 Australian consumers and small businesses across metropolitan, regional and rural communities. The research provides a comprehensive view of how Australians are experiencing, interpreting and responding to risk in an increasingly uncertain environment. View a copy of the report at iag.com.au/resilientfutures
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
